title: Simple Playhouse Deck
summary: The 8x8 raised deck platform that playhouses get built on — 4x4 posts, 2x6 framing, real joists. Build it dead level and everything on top of it goes up easy.
description:
Every backyard playhouse, fort, and clubhouse starts with the same problem: a level, solid platform on unlevel ground. This plan is that platform — an 8-foot-square deck on 4x4 corner posts, framed in 2x6 with joists on layout, diagonal corner braces, and 2x4 cross-bracing below. It's deliberately built like a real deck, because kids load structures like a demolition crew.

The honest line in the plan deserves quoting: 'it will be a miracle if it's square without adjustment.' Expect to rack it, expect to shim, and get the frame level and square BEFORE the diagonal braces go on — the braces lock in whatever geometry exists when they're fastened, correct or not.

The cut list carries the swing-set option: two of the four posts run 96" instead of 47", becoming uprights for a swing arm later. Decide before cutting, because it changes which posts land where. All screws are exterior self-tappers, everything gets paint or stain, and once the platform is done, the walls that come next are a separate (and easier) project — this deck is the part that has to be right.

tools.note: The whole project is a levelness delivery system. A 4-footer earns its keep here.
Deck boards and edges — bare feet and crawling knees live up here.

materials.name: 4x4 post, 8 ft
materials.note: Three if building the swing-set option. Ground-contact treated.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Frame, joists, and decking. Eight can be 92-5/8" studs if cheaper.

cutList.note: Swing-set option: two at 47" and two at 96".

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four posts at 47" from the 4x4. Cut eight shorter side boards at 92-1/2", four longer side boards at 96", eight diagonal corner supports at 12" and 16 deck boards at 96" from the 2x6. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 8 ft 4x4 and 30 8 ft 2x6. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Attach the first side boards to the posts
steps.body: Lay the 4x4 posts flat and screw the first side boards to them with 3" screws, per the diagram. Building the swing option? One post per side is a 96-footer — decide which end of the playhouse faces the swing before this step, not after.

steps.title: Close the box — then fight it square
steps.body:
Attach the remaining side boards to the posts with 3" screws, forming the 8x8 rim. Now the real work: level the frame on the actual ground (dig or shim under posts — dirt moves, framing doesn't) and square it by matching diagonals.

The plan promises it won't be square on the first try, and the plan is right. Take the time; every later piece inherits this geometry.

steps.title: Lock it with the diagonal braces
steps.body: With the box square AND level — verify both again — screw the eight 45°-mitered braces across the corners. These freeze the geometry: they make a square frame permanent and a crooked one permanent too. That's why they come after the fight, never before.

steps.title: Hang the joists
steps.body: Mark the joist layout on the long sides — even spacing, roughly 16" centers — and screw each joist through the rim with 3" screws, three per end, tops flush with the rim top. Sight down the tops: a proud joist telegraphs a hump into the deck.

steps.title: Lay the deck boards
steps.body:
Screw the sixteen deck boards across the joists with 2-1/2" screws, two per crossing, small even gaps for drainage. Start flush at one edge and check remaining spacing every few boards so the last one isn't a sliver.

Sand the whole surface and every arris — this deck hosts bare feet, crawling knees, and eventually someone's sleeping bag.

steps.title: Cross-brace underneath and finish
steps.body:
Screw the 2x4 cross braces under the deck per the diagram, into the framing — they stop side-to-side sway once kids discover the deck is also a stage, a ship, and a trampoline.

Paint or stain everything, then build whatever the platform was for: walls, railings, the swing arm. The hard part — flat, level, solid — is done.

images.alt: Raised 8x8 wooden deck platform on 4x4 posts, framed for a playhouse
